summary:
Only one answer fits all criteria — Option D: Nadalol. It’s safe, effective, evidence-based preventative treatment after an initial variceal bleed event involving cirrhosis and portal hypertension.

Final Answer: $\boxed{\text{D}}$

The correct choice among these agents is **nadalol**, given its proven efficacy in decreasing rates of subsequent variceal bleeds when initiated early in management of patients with alcoholic liver disease complicated by portal hypertensive gastropathy.

Note: While some may consider other drugs initially useful acutely, only nadolol serves both therapeutic purpose beyond immediate control AND provides meaningful benefit over time regarding secondary outcomes related specifically to varix stability under stress conditions typical seen within advanced stages of liver failure caused by prolonged ethanol exposure combined with structural changes
